title: Hard-crack toffee
subtitle: Brittle butter toffee at the snap stage
excerpt: British hard-crack toffee in the Bonfire Night tradition: butter, sugar and water boiled to 150°C, poured thin into a tray, set hard, and broken into shards. One large slab, broken into pieces for a sweet jar.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: documents 'Everton toffee' (hard-crack butter toffee) in the confectionery chapter. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the Yorkshire and Lancashire toffee traditions and the bonfire-night association.

content.text: British hard-crack toffee is a one-pan bake: butter, demerara sugar, golden syrup and water boiled together to 150°C (hard-crack stage), poured thin onto a lined tray, and left to set. When cool, it breaks into shards with the back of a spoon. Traditional on bonfire night with treacle parkin and roast chestnuts.

A heavy pan matters. The syrup will splutter as the water boils off, and a thin pan sends heat unevenly. The sugar thermometer is essential. Colour alone is unreliable, because demerara starts golden and goes mahogany at hard-crack.

attrs.body: The syrup reaches 150°C. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.
attrs.title: Hot syrup is dangerous

content.text: Line a rectangular tin with oiled baking paper. Combine the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{demerara-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, {{water}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a heavy saucepan over a low heat. Stir gently until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Clip the sugar thermometer to the side. Bring to a boil and cook, swirling the pan occasionally but not stirring, until the thermometer reads 150°C, this takes 12 to 15 minutes.

As soon as the syrup reaches 150°C, pour into the lined tin. Tilt the tin to spread evenly to a thin layer (about 5 mm). Leave to set at cool room temperature for an hour. Break into shards with the back of a heavy spoon. Store in an airtight tin.
